title: Sacristains
subtitle: French twisted puff pastry sticks with almonds and sugar
excerpt: Sacristains are a French boulangerie classic made from puff pastry trimmed into strips, brushed with egg white, scattered with flaked almonds and coarse sugar, then twisted along their length before baking. They come out as crisp, caramelised spirals with a light crunch and a sweet, buttery bite. A batch takes about twenty minutes start to finish if you have ready-rolled puff pastry. Often made from offcuts left over from other pastry work.
sourceNotes: Sacristains are named after the sacristain (the church officer responsible for maintaining sacred vessels) on account of their resemblance to the twisted keys sacristains traditionally carried. They appear in French pâtisserie manuals from the nineteenth century and are closely related to the arlette and the twisted palmier tradition. The name and shape appear in Escoffier's 'Le Guide Culinaire' (1903).

recipe.batchNotes: Scales straightforwardly — use more pastry and more topping.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are made. Store in an airtight tin for up to 2 days; re-crisp in a 160-degree-C oven for 5 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; high heat caramelises the sugar and puffs the pastry quickly

content.text: The twist is what holds the topping on during baking: the almonds and sugar are pressed into the pastry, then the strip is twisted from both ends so that the topping is wrapped into the spiral. If the sacristains are laid flat and the topping sits loosely on top, it slides off. Chill the twisted strips on the tray for 10 minutes before baking so the pastry is cold and firm when it hits the oven; this is what drives the lift.

Line two baking trays. Unroll the chilled puff pastry onto a lightly floured surface. Brush the entire surface with beaten egg white. Scatter 60 g flaked almonds and 40 g caster sugar evenly over the pastry. Lightly press the topping in with a rolling pin or your palm so it adheres. Cut the pastry into strips about 2 cm wide and 12 cm long. Take each strip at both ends and twist in opposite directions to form a spiral, pressing the ends firmly onto the lined tray. Space the twists about 3 cm apart. Refrigerate for 10 minutes. Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until deep golden and caramelised. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring; the caramel is molten and will stick if moved immediately. Dust with icing sugar if you like.

items.solution: The egg white was not applied generously enough, or the topping was not pressed in before twisting. Brush the egg white right to the edges and press the almonds firmly with a rolling pin before cutting.

items.problem: Pastry did not puff — sacristains are flat and oily
items.solution: The pastry was too warm when it went in the oven. Cold pastry produces steam between the laminated layers, which is what lifts it. Always chill on the tray before baking and use a properly preheated oven.

items.problem: Sacristains are soft and chewy rather than crisp
items.solution: They were underbaked. The caramel needs to colour properly to set crisp — pale sacristains will be soft. Bake until deeply golden, even if they look done early. The sugar continues to set as they cool.
